critiques.author: treapr
critiques.content:
Hmm, it's realllyyy hard to explain in 1-2 sentences as it needs a LOT of context. the few interviews i have had seemed really interested in the context and learning about this need they never knew existed

It was a startup that came out of stealth 2 months before I joined, providing an AI assistant for franchises - scrapes their corporate documentation and automatically responds to franchisee queries (which historically has long wait queues and is difficult to find information). We had 75k in sales anually from our pilot customers, and we were having a hard time getting new customers (cold emails, personal connections, e.t.c). Managers knew I had a background in web development + UI/UX design, so I took our web messenger client POC and completely overhauled it, re-designing the entire aesthetic and adding a bunch of new functionality like ability to rate responses based on usefulness, light/dark mode support, e.t.c.

They then used the new UI I designed to demo the product at two franchising expos (one in canada, one in states), and it was incredibly effective - catching the eye of 30+ franchising brands that got in touch with us. during the remaining 2 months I was there, we built sandboxes for their datasets and closed contracts with a few of them resulting in those massive sales growths

I try to include the values there to show concrete proof of some of my ui/ux design and product design skills. alternatives like "significant sales growth" feels too ambiguous and "400% increase in ARR" feels really suspicious without any relative metric (i tried getting feedback on it from a friend and they asked me if we went from $1 -> $5 in sales lol).

critiques.author: treapr
critiques.content:
Generally I want something that is front-end/fullstack based - I'm really results-driven, and like working on high-impact projects or customer-facing issues. critiques.author: whale
critiques.content:
I mean your resume is fine. You just have really high standards. Nothing wrong with that but it’s definitely not going to be easy.

In terms of first years you have a great resume. In terms of upper years it’s pretty weak but that’s to be expected. It's clear you're applying to jobs that aren't exclusive for first years so yeah. 

Also rn the job market is that even if you are good enough, it's a numbers game of hundreds of apps for a few responses.

critiques.author: whale
critiques.content: Also just keep in mind that almost no one in first year gets big corp or well known startup. There are some exceptions in terms of nepo, luck, being very very qualified, or just big companies offering first year exclusive programs which are also still hard to get and usually require you to be some form of visible minority in tech.

author: asterbot
post_message:
Hey,
I'm in 3A, looking for general SWE co-ops for S25. 
Any feedback would be appreciated :) thanks!

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
- The margins are too tight IMO. This is so tight that it won't print on most printers
- Why do you have a safety standards wiki page linked? Anybody who doesn't already know what that is won't care to learn (or at least link the actual gov webpage). The blue highlight draws the eye to this mostly irrelevant part of your resume
- What do you mean you reduced redundancy by 30% by applying SOLID? Is that 30% LoC reduction? What does script accuracy refer to? Reducing outstanding tasks by 10% is also a suspect stat (whether it means anything will depend entirely on the way your team uses jira) but at least I understand what you mean
- I personally am against using adverbs and/or describing the skills you demonstrated in your resume. E.g. "Demonstrated leadership skills by..." or "Proficiently utilized...". Tell me what you did and I'll decide how to interpret it. Those phrases are empty. I might even disagree with you about what skills you demonstrated.
- 20-30 min playtime is also a weird stat because that's more a function of how much level content you made than how many technical features you built. But I could see leaving it in
- The number of epochs over which you trained a model is meaningless on its own. Epoch isn't a well defined term so this doesn't tell me anything about the compute/data efficiency of your architecture.
- IMO 84% is a bit low of a CAV to highlight on your resume. For reference, you need an 85% CAV to be a marker for 1st year math, so that's at least 1 bar you would be DQed for. I think 3.8/4.0 is solid (because the way we convert to 4.0 scale at waterloo is kinda cheese) so you could leave that part on for external apps, especially to the US.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: I think your P2P network project description is the best one here. It avoids the vague descriptive language to be concise about what you built and uses concrete terms to describe tech/patterns involved (as opposed to listing high level design patterns like OOP which are too vague imo). Although it doesn't have any stats, which is fair for a hackathon project, I think you should try to use this style more

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
There's also enough fat to cut that you can grow your margins without losing content. E.g. your image classifier project can be redone in 1 line:
Built [deep learning model/some other descriptor] using Pytorch and Keras trained on the CIFAR-10 dataset, achieving 85% accuracy
or
Achieved 85% classification accuracy on the CIFAR-10 dataset using an ML model built with Pytorch and Keras

- multi-threaded ssh is awkward grammatically. the ssh itself isn't multithreaded, you're just running multiple concurrent ssh sessions (i assume). I would reword to "via several [concurrent or async/asynchronous] SSH sessions"
- you have a tendency to list the same technologies many times. your apple block says Java Spring Boot and AWS RDS in every single bullet. It's especially jarring when you say "accelerated ... by using Java Spring Boot, etc, ..." because you bury the lead about what you actually did (migrate stuff to an independent service) behind info i already had. My eyes initially glazed over the back half of the bullet and missed that you explained **how** you got a speedup (which is valuable info) because I saw the tech stack for the 3rd time in a row
- "Realized up 2x engineering productivity" -  grammar? not sure what this should say. realized up to 2x eng prod gain? Or you improved eng prod up to 2x of original?
- IMO putting dates on projects is a bad idea
- Sometimes you use the same phrase too close toegether. Both your algo trading bullets use "leveraging"
- You seem to want quant but C++ is your last language listed?
- Clang is kind of a nonsense thing to list as like a technology next to your matching engine. This raises a bit of a red flag in my systems engineer brain.
- Try to break up some of your 2 line points into 2 single line points. Longer sentences are just harder to read. E.g. your first IBM point is hard for me to parse. I would try "Designed distributed microservice system/architecture for handling shipment data in Java Spring Boot" and then "Achieved a 50% latency reduction when handling high volume data by leveraging Redis caching of shipment groups"

critiques.author: Ming
critiques.content:
Thanks for all the valuable feedback!

Some questions I have:
1. My nvidia internship is extended so I'm doing my final term in school while doing the internship remotely. So does that raise a red flag in company's eyes?
2. For internship projects that use a lot of the same technology, i.e. my apple internship, how would you recommend I list my technologies? Maybe I just won't put all of them all the time
3. Why is putting dates on projects a bad idea?

Once again, I really appreciate your feedback!

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
No problem
1. I don't think that's a big deal. I just pointed out the dates cause you have it as Sep 2024-Apr 2024 right now which is obviously a typo.
2. I think as long as each technology is listed in at least 1 bullet point then you're good. Java Spring Boot and AWS RDS are in the first bullet so they don't need to be in the later ones. Kafka is only in the last bullet so that's fine.
3. The way I see it is that the date doesn't add any value to the project (I don't really care when the project was built, just that you built it) but it does allow me to build a narrative about you. In your case, if I'm a citadel recruiter I might notice that you built 2 very quant-y projects within 2 months during your final year, which signals that you built those projects to get past my resume screen and not for some deep technical curiosity. If your projects were really old, I might read that as a lack of initiative/curiosity since you haven't built anything notable in a while. 9/10 times this won't matter, but it could. Tl;dr, I don't see any upside but I do see possible downside

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
- Not enough whitespace. I would cut the entire awards section to add whitespace between the remaining stuff and maybe an extra bullet or two. Not interested in certs or your hs achievements. I could see keeping the 20k schol. I feel like every international resume i see has a scholarship of that size which is why i would cut. You could squeeze it into education to save on the awards heading
- There's something floaty about the way you write bullet points. It's hard to articulate but I find myself reading much more than i'm learning. Some of it is filler language but even the technical language can feel empty. It might be that you're trying to say too much without explaining enough about any one thing.
Like what is multi-node encryption-decryption efficiency? Is everything encrypted as it passes through your pipeline? But you also have to decrypt at every step? Maybe this is some data eng-specific thing. But then how did you actually improve the efficiency of that? Snowflake S3 and Postgres are the basic components of the pipeline, they don't explain the efficiency gain.
Or what was the bounding box and text generation actually for? How does that "enhance document analysis accuracy and efficiency"?
What did your Azure classification model categorize users for? Why do I care that you used "curated datasets"?
What am I supposed to take away from the fact that MongoDB handled "user data, transactions, and biofuel plant information"? A DB stores data, and that's just data. The transition from vague "user data" to "biofuel plant info" is also jarring
- I wouldn't bold niche technologies like Instamojo
- reducing security risks by 40% sounds kinda sus. i feel like you can only say 40% if you know the total number of vulnerabilities in your system. So then why are 60% of them still there? Saying you fixed some specific number of vulnerabilities would be fine.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: You also use analyses of your work which are unexplained/unearned. How did your use of Mongo "ensure data integrity and security"? How did your CI/CD pipelines enhance dev efficiency? What made your Chess GUI "cohesive"?

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
Pretty solid quantity of experience for 1b but communication is kinda sloppy
- Your stats are suspect to me. Reduced formatting time by 75% compared to what? Traditional OCR does not parse equations afaik so how are you comparing your accuracy to it? Improved search latency *and efficiency* by 40%? What is efficiency? I also don't really like the Selenium stat because you probably haven't actually measured the manual search time but not the worst. Looks bad in context
- I feel like there's a lot more to say about LaTeX.ly than the tech stack and dataset size. Parsing images -> latex equations sounds like an architecturally distinct problem from normal OCR since you can't just identify characters in a stream. If there's anything to say about how you made it understand fractions or subexpressions in exponents/subscripts, you should say it
- Looquest is unclear. You're semantic searching over course topics? What's the graph for? What did you actually do to optimize postgres perf?
- The last linkedit bullet after Won Geeshacks is redundant
- Not a fan of descriptive statements like "for seamless equation..." or "streamline sponsor discovery..". It's filler but ok if you need to fill space
I think you've overindexed on cramming stats and keywords over clearly explaining what you built + why it was valuable/interesting. You should even have enough space to do both if you cut from awards, debate club, and fluff

- 100% of cart data is a weird thing to bold. maybe it was a hard technical problem figuring out how to ingest/use 100% of the data but it means nothing to me. cool project tho. might want to invert it a little and say "built a chrome extension that launches an ace attorney style debate... by interecepting amazon/shopify". my eyes kinda glazed over by the time i got the AI lawyer debate midway thru the 2nd line but that's the fun part.

author: awaterloostudent
post_message: 2B CS looking for SWE internships (primarily backend) for summer 2026. Any advice is appreciated.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
you worked on "prediction markets" trading lol? or were you working for a sportsbook (i.e. bet pricing algorithms). just curious.
- "bachelors of math, major in cs" may be technically correct if you're in bmath cs but buries the lead. lead with the cs part for most jobs. "[bachelor of/major in] cs, minor in stats"
- "Designed and deployed MLB trading algorithm". this phrase carries a lot of weight to me. you designed the strategy? how good is it? or did you just deploy it? also MLB like you're literally trading prop bets on MLB games right? I hope that would be clear if i knew the company name. otherwise the idea that you wrote an system to do sports betting would be pretty foreign to a lot of people (maybe offputting). i would try my best to divorce the tech stuff that you did from the product you were trading so i don't have to mention it was sports betting.
- Your second "in-game algo" bullet is vague. what latency are you measuring? tick to trade? 400ms tick to trade seems pretty bad but depends on the model/time-horizon/product. also, don't just tell me you used concurrency, caching, and a tuned http client (not sure what that one means). try to say a few words about what was concurrent or cached. something like "engineered pipeline that concurrently ingests and evaluates market data" might be one bullet and "cached xyz, acheiving 400ms latency" might be another
- "Integrated XGBoost pricing model by compiling lightweight Treelite object and linking via cgo into Go algorithm" ngl but this sounds like 2 hours of work. you compiled a model and linked into it.
- what does bigquery have to do with the algorithm? how did we get to 80% faster predictions?

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
sorry this is too late for ww cycle 1. just got around to this
I think the structure got better. i just have some minor gripes about wording:
- ML evaluation to me means evaluating the performance/accuracy of models. I would stick to saying inference
- poll-to-intent throughput should be poll-to-intent latency. you're still measuring the time it takes to perform 1 action, not how many actions you can do in a fixed time
- "standalone pre-event forecasts" is an awkward phrase. I would just drop standalone and say "pre-event forecasts". then you have room to say "by 80% by unifying..." instead of just a comma
- "Powered ML evaluation by designing" seems pointless. "Designed/built/trained XGBoost pricing model and optimized inference using Treelite and cgo" is better to me
- i think first bullet reads better as "Developed an automated trading system for event-driven markets and validated its integrity in live deployment."

- your bullet points are too long. longer sentences tend to be harder to parse. my heuristic is you should only have a small handful of 2-liners in your whole resume. easy first step is replace semicolons with line breaks
- From Xms to Yms is weak framing unless the numbers are really bold. prefer multiples/proportions. 150ms to 1ms sounds sick but don't make me do 1300/240 in my head to figure out how significant that was 
- do you have a better name for the accelerator you used for FRC than a TPU? i read that as google TPU but ik it's not that if you had this thing deployed on a FRC bot

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
generally, i think there's a lot of fat here. i normally complain that people are too vague ("collaborated with 15 people in an AGILE team"). you include a lot of implementation details, which is an improvement, but is still sometimes irrelevant and obscures more interesting details about what you did. as an exercise, here's my inline comments on your 2 experiences (strikethrough -> redundant imo):
> Implemented a dual-agent architecture: one agent streams fast replies, while a second agent performs memory/RAG
> update and search ~~asynchronously and communicates via RPC~~, reducing response latency from 500ms to 80ms.
> • Partnered with the Letta (MemGPT) engineering team ~~to test a real-time memory SDK~~ **(maybe ok if the SDK is well-known)** for voice agents; identified
> and parallelized a RAG performance bottleneck, cutting latency from 1300ms to 240ms **(what latency is this? how is this different from the last one?)**.
> • Implemented real-time avatar lip-sync and expression rendering by building an audio-to-phoneme mapping pipeline
> ~~and driving mouth/expression parameters from live audio, enabling synchronized real-time virtual chatbot avatars.~~
> • Enabled fully autonomous navigation~~ + scoring~~ for ~~2025 ~~FRC through an AprilTag pose pipeline to transform
> robot-relative pose to global field coordinates~~ via rotational and translational matrices,~~ achieving ±5 inch pose error.
> • Built a vision-guided ~~auto-~~intake system ~~for the 2024 FRC game~~ by deploying a detection model on a TPU processor
> and converting bounding-box detections into robot-relative pose targets for autonomous pickup, winning Innovation in
> Control ~~(Humber College event)~~.
> • Optimized ~~2024 game-piece~~ vision with an OpenCV pipeline (~~color thresholding + ellipse detection~~) while preserving
> detection quality **(measured how? vague)**, cutting latency from 300ms to 120ms and eliminating the need for the TPU processor **(why was this better than TPU?)**.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
- What is "technical deep dives"? Why is this not just in your skills? Half of those technologies were already mentioned in depth in the resume body too
- Your line spacing is kinda fucked. Some lines are closer together than others.
- Can you make it less cramped? I would completely cut Teranet to give me more breathing room in your spacing and margins
- Some of your bullets get too into the weeds. Try to avoid using language that's too technical or domain specific. I think "Reduced Raft's latency by up to 50%" and "Eliminated up to 3 seconds of db unavailability during fail-overs" communicates all the same stuff in simpler language. Only DB engineers would notice the difference. Also in your version you use present tense for one and past tense for the other. There are a lot of dead words that add a tiny bit of detail at a much larger cost to readability. Another strategy is inverting your bullets so you lead with impact. Your Auvik Kubernetes point should lead with "streamlined..." and then say "by leveraging...". Somebody who's not that familiar with Kubernetes can understand the impact you had and then ignore the details at the end instead of skipping that bullet after hitting the dense technical jargon
Overall this is one of the top resumes i've seen on here. Get a good sense of what you can do, why your work was important, why it was cool. Just needs some clarity

Are there any standard technologies you can talk about from your work? I'm thinking there might be something there in your game startup A one cause there's some web stuff

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: I personally really like this resume. Sounds like you've done a pretty wide variety of interesting stuff. And written clearly. But you're kind of raw material as a candidate. I would hire you because I think you have strong fundamentals, not because you know anything about my particular tech stack. I feel like that's a hard sell to a lot of big tech, especially vs people whos resumes are loaded with full-stack experience who would be more plug and play.
